notes: Iran's economy grew 33% in 1973, described as the world's fastest-growing economy, sustained for over a decade.
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.2

notes: Growth expected to accelerate to 40% in 1974, driven by the recent oil-price surge.
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.2

notes: Iran's GNP of about $40 billion described as more than four times that of nearest Middle East rivals Egypt and Israel.
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.2

subcategory: Fifth Development Plan spending, originally planned
notes: Fifth Plan (1973-77) development spending originally planned at $23 billion before being expanded.
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.2

subcategory: Fifth Development Plan spending, expanded total
notes: Development spending expanded from the original $23 billion plan to $45 billion during the Fifth Plan.
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.2

subcategory: Expected annual oil revenue, rest of decade
notes: The Shah could count on oil revenues of some $20 billion annually through the rest of the decade absent a sharp price reversal, to finance development plans.
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.2

notes: In 1974 the Shah committed more than $8 billion to foreign lending and investment abroad (advances to France/UK, Krupp steel stake, aid credits to India, loans to Egypt/Syria/African nations) to recycle surplus oil funds.
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.2

notes: About $8 billion spent on armaments in 1967-73, the largest arms buildup in the Middle East, aimed at Persian Gulf dominance and Indian Ocean influence.
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.3

notes: Contract signed for $600 million for US F-14 fighters armed with Phoenix air-to-air missiles.
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.3

notes: Price increase of 6% in 1972, cited as a baseline before the 1973-74 acceleration.
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.3

notes: Price increase of 11% in 1973.
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.3

notes: Prices during the first quarter of 1974 ran at an annual rate of 16%, attributed to large government outlays and enormous capital inflow.
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.3

notes: Despite large government spending, the top 20% of the population probably still received over half of total income.
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.3

notes: The lowest 20% of the population received only about 5% of total income -- paired with the top-20% figure to show persistent inequality despite the oil boom.
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.3

notes: Tehran plans to invest $6 billion in industrial expansion during the current (Fifth) Plan, an opportunity area for US business (design/equipment/installation).
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.3

subcategory: Planned investment in oil and gas development
notes: Tehran plans to invest $5 billion in oil and gas development during the current Plan.
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.3

subcategory: Iranian imports of goods and services incl. arms, expected annual level
notes: Iranian imports of goods and services, including arms, expected to swell to about $10 billion annually by 1975.
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.3

notes: The $10 billion import level was expected to result in US sales of at least $1.6 billion, with the US needing to counter growing Japanese/West European competition to retain share.
citation: CIA/OER, "Iran: An Overview of the Shah's Economy" (S-6549), 16 October 1974, PDF p.3

subcategory: Iran average oil export level, last year (1985)
notes: Assumed baseline: Tehran maintained last year's (1985) average oil export level of about 1.6 million b/d.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.3

subcategory: Assumed oil price for 1986 FX-earnings estimate
notes: Estimate assumes an oil price of $13 per barrel.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.3

subcategory: Iran estimated drop in real foreign exchange earnings
notes: Assuming a $13/barrel price and no significant dollar appreciation, Iran faces a 50-55 percent drop in real foreign exchange earnings in 1986.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.3

subcategory: Iran intended oil export increase target
notes: Iran intends to increase oil exports to between 2 and 2.8 million b/d, though shortages of skilled personnel and facility deterioration will make this hard to sustain.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.3

subcategory: Iran oil exports, first four months of 1986 average
notes: Exports for the first four months of 1986 averaged 1.5 million b/d.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.3

subcategory: Oil share of Iran's foreign exchange earnings
notes: Oil revenues constitute over 90 percent of Iran's foreign exchange earnings.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.3

subcategory: Oil-funded imports share of Iran's food requirements
notes: Oil-funded imports provide 20-25 percent of Iran's food requirements, plus the vast bulk of its war materiel and essential industrial/transport/construction supplies.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.3

notes: Estimated Iranian foreign exchange revenue for 1986: $8.5 billion; maintaining recent food/military import levels would use almost three-fourths of this.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.3

notes: Tehran will need to slash imports by one-third in 1986 despite expected foreign loans from Japan and West European trading partners.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.3

notes: A one-third 1986 cut would represent about a 50 percent decline in Iran's imports since 1984.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.3

notes: Iran's limited foreign exchange assets amount to about three months of imports, serving as a cushion against a complete oil-export cutoff -- probably will avoid drawing these down.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.3

notes: Official Iranian estimates place unemployment at 15 percent, or about 2 million people.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.4

notes: Official estimate of approximately 2 million unemployed.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.4

subcategory: Iran 'false employment' + unemployment share of labor force
notes: Former head of Iran's Planning and Budget Ministry complained in April 1986 that "false employment" (government make-work) plus unemployment account for 39 percent of Iran's 12.3 million labor force, per press reports.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.4

notes: Iran's labor force cited at 12.3 million as of this 1986 assessment.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.4

notes: Iraqi foreign exchange earnings estimated to fall to $7.4 billion in 1986.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.4

notes: Iraqi foreign exchange earnings in 1985 were $11.7 billion, the prior-year baseline for the 1986 decline.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.4

notes: Baghdad will need to cut imports by 30-35 percent given the FX earnings decline; deeper if Saudi Arabia/Kuwait do not provide additional cash assistance.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.4

subcategory: Oil exports share of Iraq's foreign exchange earnings
notes: Iraqi oil exports, already near capacity, represent 98 percent of foreign exchange earnings -- Baghdad cannot appreciably raise revenues.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.4

subcategory: Iraq missed payment to France (first-time default)
notes: US Embassy in Baghdad reports Iraq failed for the first time to make a $120 million payment to France, its major creditor and an important arms supplier; Paris suspended short/medium-term credit as a result.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.5

notes: Baghdad's failure to honor up to $1 billion in letters of credit -- the principal manner of conducting trade -- caused many banks to refuse handling Iraqi L/Cs.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.5

subcategory: Iraq debt payments sought for rescheduling
notes: Iraq reportedly seeking to reschedule at least $3 billion in debt payments due in 1986.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.5

subcategory: Iraq required FX share to maintain military/food imports
notes: To maintain recent import levels of military goods and food, Iraq would have to use almost 90 percent of its estimated 1986 foreign exchange revenues.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.5

notes: Iraq had about 1.2 million foreign workers in 1985; the regime is trying to reduce this number despite a war-intensified labor shortage.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.5

notes: Tehran has about $3 billion in readily accessible foreign exchange assets, versus almost none for Baghdad.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.7

subcategory: Iraq debt owed to non-Arab creditors
notes: Iraq owes $9-10 billion to non-Arab debtors and has virtually no access to further credit, versus Iran's negligible foreign debt.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.7

subcategory: New Iraq pipeline through Turkey completion
notes: A new Iraqi oil pipeline through Turkey is scheduled for completion in May 1987, expected to raise export capacity.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.7

subcategory: Oil sector share of domestic output, Iraq
notes: Two-thirds of Iraq's domestic output is directly linked to oil.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.7

subcategory: Oil sector share of domestic output, Iran
notes: The oil sector contributes only about one-third of domestic output in Iran, versus two-thirds for Iraq.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.7

subcategory: Saudi/Kuwait financial support to Iraq since war start, cumulative
notes: About $25 billion in financial support from Saudi Arabia and Kuwait since the beginning of the war has helped maintain Iraqi living standards.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.7

subcategory: Decline in Iraq oil revenues this year
notes: Total Arab aid this year is unlikely to offset the $4.3 billion decline in Iraqi oil revenues unless substantial additional cash payments are made.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.7

subcategory: Saudi/Kuwait financial assistance to Iraq, 1985 level
notes: Financial assistance from Saudi Arabia and Kuwait for 1986 will probably be even less than last year's (1985) $2.0-2.5 billion, absent substantial additional cash payments.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.7

subcategory: Iraq oil aid figure announced by Baghdad
notes: The 300,000 b/d oil-aid figure announced by Baghdad (crude sold on Iraq's behalf, mainly via the Neutral Zone) may be overstated because of low Neutral Zone production.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.7

subcategory: Neutral Zone share of Iraq's oil aid
notes: Low production in the Neutral Zone, which accounts for 80 percent of Iraq's oil aid, may make the 300,000 b/d aid figure overstated.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.7

notes: Saudi Arabia has restricted Iraqi crude flow through the Iraq-Saudi pipeline to about 150,000 b/d below its 500,000 b/d capacity, depriving Baghdad of several hundred million dollars since last November (1985).
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.7

notes: Full nameplate capacity of the Iraq-Saudi pipeline, against which the ~150,000 b/d restriction is measured.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.7

subcategory: Iranian tanker attacks on Saudi shipping
notes: In early May 1986 Iran attacked three Saudi tankers in the Gulf to underscore its warning that Saudi Arabia/Kuwait should restrain oil production and discontinue economic support for Iraq.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA M 86-20082, "Iran-Iraq: The Economic Balance", 6 June 1986, PDF p.6

notes: Iraq's proved oil reserves increased this year to 100 billion barrels -- second in the world only to Saudi Arabia's.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.iii/1

notes: Assuming oil prices of $13-16/barrel, Iraq could earn $15-20 billion in oil revenues annually by 1990 (highest since 1980) on exports of about 3.3 million b/d.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.iii

subcategory: Iraq projected annual oil revenue by 1990
notes: Iraq could earn $15-20 billion in oil revenues annually by 1990 -- the highest level since 1980 -- given the assumed price/export scenario.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.iii

subcategory: Iraq non-Arab military and civilian debt
notes: Servicing Iraq's $40 billion non-Arab military and civilian debt will probably be Baghdad's major postwar challenge.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.iii

notes: Iran's current oil production capacity of roughly 3 million b/d is about half what it was 10 years ago (i.e. under the Shah).
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.iii

subcategory: Iran population when the Shah fell
notes: Iran's population was 37 million when the Shah fell (1978/79), used as the baseline for the war-era population surge.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.iv

notes: Rapid population growth swelled Iran's population from about 37 million (1978) to 52 million by midyear 1988.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.iv

notes: Iraq's heavy borrowing caused its non-Arab military and civilian debt to rise from about $5 billion in 1979 to $40 billion in 1987, funding both military and civilian imports Tehran could not match.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.1

subcategory: Interest-free 'soft' loans to Iraq from Arab Gulf allies since 1980
notes: Iraq received $35 billion in interest-free 'soft' loans since 1980 from its Arab allies in the Persian Gulf (mainly Saudi Arabia and Kuwait), via Neutral Zone crude oil sales on Iraq's behalf and cash payments.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.1

notes: Iraq's foreign assets, including foreign exchange reserves and gold, fell from $36 billion in 1980 to about $3 billion in 1987.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.1

notes: In 1987, Iraq's civilian imports amounted to $7.1 billion -- roughly half the 1980 level -- as Baghdad restricted civilian imports to essentials like food and medicine plus defense/oil-industry materials.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.1

notes: Iran's foreign assets fell from about $19 billion in 1980 to $5.5 billion 'last year' (1987), of which only an estimated $1.5 billion are liquid reserves.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.1

subcategory: Iran liquid reserves within total foreign assets
notes: Of Iran's $5.5 billion in foreign assets, only an estimated $1.5 billion are liquid reserves.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.1

notes: Iranian civilian imports in 1986 -- about $8 billion -- were nearly 35 percent less than in 1980 despite a near-20-percent increase in population over the period.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.1

notes: Iran's 1986 civilian imports were nearly 35 percent below the 1980 level despite population growth of nearly 20 percent over the same period.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.1

notes: Plus $575 a month, apparently for life, alongside the lump sum; additional durable-goods benefits if the martyr left a large family.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.4

subcategory: Iran unemployment insurance, maximum monthly payment
notes: In August 1987, Iran's Ministry of Labor established unemployment insurance entitling some laid-off employees to monthly payments of up to $575.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.4

notes: Small community banks associated with mosques ("Funds for the Lending of Money for Good Purposes") sprang up nationwide, offering loans of up to $30,000 with minimal red tape.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.4

subcategory: Oil sector share of Iran GNP, revolution-era peak
notes: Iran's oil sector has shrunk from at least 38 percent of GNP at the beginning of the revolution to less than 10 percent in 1987.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.4

subcategory: Oil sector share of Iran GNP, 1987
notes: By 1987 the oil sector had fallen to less than 10 percent of Iran's GNP, reflecting falling oil export revenues rather than nonoil sector gains.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.4

subcategory: Crude oil share of Iran's hard-currency earnings
notes: Crude oil sales still account for at least 90 percent of Iran's hard currency earnings despite nonoil exports (carpets, textiles, pistachios) almost doubling their SHARE of total exports since 1985.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.5

notes: Foreign sales of Iranian nonoil goods have earned almost a billion dollars annually in recent years.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.5

subcategory: Oil export revenue share of Iraq's export earnings
notes: Iraq's oil export revenues account for about 95 percent of its export earnings.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.5

subcategory: Oil production share of Iraq GNP, prewar
notes: Oil production accounted for about 50 percent of Iraq's GNP in 1979-80, before the war.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.5

subcategory: Oil production share of Iraq GNP, 1987
notes: By 1987 oil production accounted for roughly 40 percent of Iraq's GNP, reflecting reduced oil production and lower prices rather than real nonoil-sector gains.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.5

subcategory: Iraq oil exports after Gulf terminal closures/Syria pipeline closure
notes: Closing of Iraq's Gulf terminals (1980) and Syria's closure of the Iraq-Syria pipeline (1982) slashed Iraqi oil exports to less than 1 million b/d.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.6

notes: Iraq increased the capacity of its pipeline through Turkey from about 700,000 b/d to 1 million b/d in 1984.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.6

notes: Iraq-Turkey pipeline capacity reached 1 million b/d in 1984.

subcategory: Iraq-Turkey pipeline capacity after further 1987 expansion
notes: The Iraq-Turkey pipeline line was expanded to 1.5 million b/d in 1987.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.6

subcategory: Iraq-Saudi (Petroline) pipeline link to Yanbu, initial capacity
notes: Baghdad began exporting oil through the 500,000 b/d pipeline link to Saudi Arabia's Petroline to its Red Sea port at Yanbu al Bahr in 1985.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.6

subcategory: Iraqi-Saudi pipeline capacity after phase-two expansion
notes: Phase two of the Iraqi-Saudi pipeline (construction begun 1987) will increase the line's capacity to 1.6 million b/d.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.6

subcategory: Iraq total export capacity after pipeline expansions, near-prewar level
notes: The pipeline expansions will enable Iraq to export near-prewar levels of 3.5 million b/d.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.6

subcategory: Iran oil production capacity decline vs. Shah-era peak
notes: Tehran's neglect resulted in a large reduction of oil production capacity from the roughly 6 million b/d reached under the Shah (to the ~3 million b/d cited in the Key Judgments).

subcategory: Abadan refinery capacity destroyed by Iraq early in the war
notes: Iraq destroyed Iran's 500,000 b/d refinery at Abadan early in the war.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.7

subcategory: Iran forced refined-product imports due to refinery war damage
notes: Additional Iraqi strikes against Iran's remaining refineries have forced Iran to import roughly 200,000 b/d of refined products.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.7

subcategory: Iraq oil export capacity when spot price dipped below $9/barrel
notes: Iraq's oil export capacity was 1.8 million b/d in 1986 when spot prices dipped below $9 per barrel.

notes: By mid-1988 Iraqi export capacity had risen to 2.3 million b/d.

subcategory: Iraq projected further export capacity rise from IPSA-2 pipeline
notes: Iraqi export capacity is expected to rise by another 1.1 million b/d in early 1990 when the IPSA-2 pipeline through Saudi Arabia is completed.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.8

notes: About 2,100 kilometers of new highways were completed in Iraq between 1980 and 1986.

notes: Figure 10 pie chart, Iraq side: Western Europe supplied 11% of Iraq's 1985-87 military imports.
citation: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 10 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Military Imports, 1985-87" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21

subcategory: Iraq military imports source share -- Others
notes: Figure 10 pie chart, Iraq side: Others supplied 17% of Iraq's 1985-87 military imports.
citation: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 10 "Iran-Iraq: Sources of Military Imports, 1985-87" (percent, pie chart with printed values), November 1988, PDF p.21

subcategory: Soviet Bloc share of Iraq arms purchases since 1979
notes: Bloc countries have supplied 60 percent of Baghdad's arms purchases since 1979, and delivered about 80 percent in 1987.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16

subcategory: Soviet Bloc share of Iraq arms deliveries, 1987
notes: The Bloc delivered about 80 percent of Iraq's arms in 1987.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16

subcategory: USSR share of Iraq arms deliveries, 1987
notes: The USSR alone, which delivered 63 percent of Iraq's arms last year (1987), supplied most of the Bloc share.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16

subcategory: Iraq debt to the Soviet Bloc
notes: Iraq's debt to the Bloc -- mostly to the USSR for military purchases -- was about $7.5 billion at yearend 1987.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16

subcategory: Soviet-claimed involvement in Iraq development projects
notes: In December 1987 the USSR claimed to be involved in nearly 100 projects in Iraq, per the US Embassy in Baghdad, though many are believed on hold due to Iraq's financial constraints.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16

subcategory: Soviet Bloc share of Iraq civilian imports
notes: The Bloc has supplied about 10 percent of Iraq's civilian imports in the 1980s.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16

subcategory: East European (esp. Romania/Bulgaria) share of Bloc civilian sales to Iraq
notes: East European countries -- especially Romania and Bulgaria -- have made about 90 percent of Bloc sales of civilian goods to Iraq (vs. the USSR dominating military sales).
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16

subcategory: Iraq foreign military+civilian debt by source, total (yearend 1987, excl. $35bn Gulf Arab soft debt)
notes: Figure 13 pie chart total: $40 billion in Iraqi foreign military and civilian debt at yearend 1987, EXCLUDING $35 billion in soft debt owed to Gulf Arab countries.
citation: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 13 "Iraq: Foreign Military and Civilian Debt by Source, Yearend 1987", November 1988, PDF p.23

notes: Figure 13 pie chart: Soviet Bloc accounts for 12% of Iraq's $40 billion foreign military/civilian debt at yearend 1987 (West subtotal = government 37 + commercial banks 20 + private firms 18 = 75%).
citation: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 13 "Iraq: Foreign Military and Civilian Debt by Source, Yearend 1987" (percent of $40bn total, excl. $35bn Gulf Arab soft debt), November 1988, PDF p.23

notes: Figure 13 pie chart: Third World accounts for 13% of Iraq's $40 billion foreign military/civilian debt at yearend 1987 (West subtotal = government 37 + commercial banks 20 + private firms 18 = 75%).
citation: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 13 "Iraq: Foreign Military and Civilian Debt by Source, Yearend 1987" (percent of $40bn total, excl. $35bn Gulf Arab soft debt), November 1988, PDF p.23

notes: Figure 13 pie chart: West -- government accounts for 37% of Iraq's $40 billion foreign military/civilian debt at yearend 1987 (West subtotal = government 37 + commercial banks 20 + private firms 18 = 75%).
citation: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 13 "Iraq: Foreign Military and Civilian Debt by Source, Yearend 1987" (percent of $40bn total, excl. $35bn Gulf Arab soft debt), November 1988, PDF p.23

subcategory: Iraq debt-by-source share -- West -- commercial banks
notes: Figure 13 pie chart: West -- commercial banks accounts for 20% of Iraq's $40 billion foreign military/civilian debt at yearend 1987 (West subtotal = government 37 + commercial banks 20 + private firms 18 = 75%).
citation: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 13 "Iraq: Foreign Military and Civilian Debt by Source, Yearend 1987" (percent of $40bn total, excl. $35bn Gulf Arab soft debt), November 1988, PDF p.23

subcategory: Iraq debt-by-source share -- West -- private firms
notes: Figure 13 pie chart: West -- private firms accounts for 18% of Iraq's $40 billion foreign military/civilian debt at yearend 1987 (West subtotal = government 37 + commercial banks 20 + private firms 18 = 75%).
citation: CIA NESA 88-10067, Figure 13 "Iraq: Foreign Military and Civilian Debt by Source, Yearend 1987" (percent of $40bn total, excl. $35bn Gulf Arab soft debt), November 1988, PDF p.23

subcategory: Soviet imports of Iranian natural gas, 1970s
notes: In the 1970s, Moscow imported some 10 billion cubic meters per year of Iranian natural gas, before price disputes virtually terminated the sales.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16

notes: Iranian-Soviet trade remained small -- about $200 million annually -- compared with Iran's Western trade, despite 1987 meetings to improve ties.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.16

subcategory: Cost estimate for USSR-Persian Gulf rail link or new Iran gas export pipeline
notes: Larger schemes such as a USSR-to-Persian-Gulf rail link or renewed Iranian gas exports (requiring a new pipeline) would cost at least $1 billion each -- an amount Iran cannot, and Moscow will not, provide.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.17

subcategory: Iran trade with Eastern Europe, annual value
notes: Iranian trade with Eastern Europe amounted to about $1 billion in 1987 -- about 5 percent of Iran's total trade.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.17

subcategory: Eastern Europe share of Iran's total trade
notes: Iran-East Europe trade represented about 5 percent of Iran's total trade in 1987.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.17

subcategory: Iraq projected annual oil revenue by 1990 (Key Judgments restated in outlook section)
notes: Restated in the outlook section: Iraq's oil revenues could reach $15-20 billion annually by 1990 because of increased oil export capacity.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.19

subcategory: Iraq annual principal + interest payments due, next five years
notes: Iraq faces $6 billion in principal and interest payments due each year for at least the next five years -- Baghdad probably will not institute severe austerity to free up resources for this.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.19

subcategory: Iran investment needed to restore per-capita GNP to 10-years-earlier level
notes: The Iranian economy needs tens of billions of dollars of investment to return its per capita gross national product to levels achieved 10 years earlier (i.e. pre-revolution/pre-war).
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.19

subcategory: Iran investment needed to reverse oil-industry erosion since the revolution
notes: It could easily take $15-30 billion to begin reversing the erosion in Iran's oil industry that has occurred since the revolution.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 88-10067, "Iran-Iraq: A Comparison of Two War-Weary Economies" (An Intelligence Assessment, information as of 10 Nov 1988), November 1988, PDF p.19

subcategory: Iraq foreign workers reduced by currency-remittance curbs
notes: Regulations curtailing the amount of money foreign workers were allowed to send out of Iraq caused about 200,000 of Iraq's 1.2 million foreign workers to leave, adversely affecting service industries and agriculture.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.8

subcategory: Iraq foreign workers, baseline total before the remittance-curb exodus
notes: Baseline: Iraq had about 1.2 million foreign workers before curbs on remittances caused roughly 200,000 (mostly Egyptian) to leave.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.8

subcategory: Iran oil exports, 1987 estimate under moderate Iraqi air pressure
notes: With only moderate Iraqi pressure through air attacks, Iranian oil exports in 1987 are estimated to average about 1.4 million b/d, somewhat less than in 1986.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.9

subcategory: Iran oil exports during intensive Iraqi air attacks, Aug-Oct 1986
notes: If Iraq carries out repeated, systematic attacks, Iranian oil exports would be kept to no more than 1 million b/d, as during the intensive air attacks of August-October 1986.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.9

notes: With little or no Iraqi military pressure on Iran's oil exports/production, Iran would produce slightly above its OPEC quota of 2.25 million b/d, implying exports of 1.6-1.7 million b/d.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.9

subcategory: Iran implied oil export range at full OPEC-quota production
notes: Producing slightly above the 2.25 million b/d OPEC quota would imply Iranian oil exports of 1.6-1.7 million b/d absent Iraqi military pressure.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.9

subcategory: Assumed Iranian crude oil price, 1987 estimate
notes: Tehran's 1987 oil export revenue estimate assumes oil prices average $15 per barrel for Iranian crude.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.9

notes: Under the $15/barrel, 1.4 million b/d export scenario, Iran's 1987 imports are expected to be about 20 percent lower than 1986 as Iran maintains the deep import cuts begun mid-1986.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.9

date_label: 1987 (scenario a: no Iraqi pressure/worst-case low prices combined in chart footnotes)
subcategory: Iran-Iraq oil export revenue chart scenario notes
notes: Figure 6 plots Iran/Iraq oil export revenues 1978-1987 as a line chart with only gridlines (no printed per-year data labels) except for the four 1987 scenario footnotes: (a) estimated; (b) oil price of $18/barrel; (c) oil price of $15/barrel; (d) oil price of $10/barrel. Left blank per no-guessing rule -- chart values not pixel-estimated; exact 1987 scenario dollar figures are captured separately via Table 1 (Iran) and Table 3 (Iraq) current-account tables on pp.14 and 18.
citation: CIA NESA 87-10025, Figure 6 "Iran-Iraq Oil Export Revenues, 1978-87" (line chart, billion US$; scenario years 87a/87b/87c/87d), May 1987, PDF p.10

subcategory: Free oil provided by Iran to Syria, continued at 1986 level
notes: Free oil provided to Syria will continue at 1986 levels of about 35,000 b/d, worth about $190 million annually at an average price of $15 per barrel.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.10

subcategory: Value of Iran's free-oil subsidy to Syria, annualized
notes: The ~35,000 b/d of free oil to Syria is worth about $190 million annually, based on an average price of $15 per barrel.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.10

subcategory: Cost of Iran's oil shuttle (maintenance, charter fees, insurance)
notes: The cost of Iran's oil shuttle, which includes maintenance, charter fees, and insurance, is estimated at at least $200 million a year.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.10

notes: Iran's nonoil exports are estimated to rise by about 25 percent in 1987 but will still account for less than 15 percent of total export revenues.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.10

subcategory: Iran nonoil exports share of total export revenues, 1987
notes: Nonoil exports will still account for less than 15 percent of Iran's total export revenues in 1987 despite the projected 25 percent rise.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.10

subcategory: Share of Iran export revenues turned over to the central bank
notes: About four-fifths of Iran's export revenues are turned over to the central bank, according to the regime's own data.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.10

subcategory: Iran readily accessible official cash reserves, start of 1987
notes: Iran began 1987 with $2-2.5 billion in readily accessible official cash reserves.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.11

subcategory: Iran gold, silver, and jewel reserves, start of 1987
notes: In addition to cash reserves, Iran held $2.3-2.5 billion in gold, silver, and jewels at the start of 1987.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.11

subcategory: Iran claimed less-liquid assets (escrow accounts, uncollectable LDC loans)
notes: Tehran claims about $6 billion in less liquid assets, including funds frozen in escrow accounts and uncollectable loans to less developed nations.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.11

subcategory: Iran annual foreign-exchange spending on military equipment/supplies
notes: Iran spends about $4 billion annually in foreign exchange for military equipment and supplies.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.11

notes: Slack domestic production and pressure to maintain supplies to the lower class probably will prompt Tehran to import $2.5-3 billion in food, medicines, and agricultural inputs in 1987.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.11

notes: Iran imported about $500 million in refined petroleum products in 1986, which could double in 1987 if Iraq intensifies attacks on refineries.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.11

subcategory: Iran oil revenue estimate, 1987 (assuming 1.4m b/d at $15/barrel)
notes: Assuming oil exports of 1.4 million b/d at an average price of $15 per barrel, Iran's 1987 oil revenues will be approximately $7.7 billion -- barely enough to cover requirements for food, fuel, and military supplies.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.12

subcategory: Iran real per-capita GNP decline, 1987 estimate
notes: On the basis of the middle estimate of oil production and prices, Iran's real per capita GNP is estimated to decline about 8 percent in 1987, following an estimated 16-percent decline in 1986.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.12

subcategory: Iran real per-capita GNP decline, 1986 estimate
notes: Iran's real per capita GNP is estimated to have declined about 16 percent in 1986, the prior-year baseline for the 1987 estimate.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.12

notes: Inflation on the black market is estimated at 20 to 25 percent in 1987, following a jump of at least 40 percent in 1986.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.12

notes: Black-market inflation jumped at least 40 percent in 1986, the prior-year baseline for the 1987 estimate.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.12

notes: Strong economic pressures will probably force the government to allow official prices to rise by about 15 percent in 1987, following increases of only about 10 percent the prior year.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.14

subcategory: Iran official price increase, prior year (1986)
notes: Official prices rose only about 10 percent in 1986, the baseline against which the 1987 ~15 percent increase is measured.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.14

subcategory: Tehran-area families in single-room housing, ghetto areas
notes: The Iranian press reports almost two-thirds of the families in Tehran's sprawling outskirt ghettos live in single, small rooms.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.14

notes: The poor quality of housing helps explain Tehran's officially estimated 6,000 fires annually.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.14

notes: Increased oil exports and higher prices are estimated to raise Iraq's oil export revenues in 1987 to about $9.6 billion, an increase of 35 percent from 1986.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.15

subcategory: Iraq oil export revenue increase, 1987 vs 1986
notes: The estimated $9.6 billion in 1987 Iraqi oil export revenue represents a 35 percent increase from 1986.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.15

subcategory: Assumed Iraqi oil export volume for the 1987 revenue estimate
notes: The 1987 Iraqi oil revenue estimate assumes Iraqi exports average 1.7-1.8 million barrels per day at $15 per barrel.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.15

subcategory: Planned expansion of Iraq's 1-million-b/d Turkey pipeline
notes: The boost in oil exports from 1986 will come from a 500,000 b/d expansion of Iraq's 1-million-b/d pipeline through Turkey, likely to be completed in fall 1987.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.15

subcategory: Saudi restriction on Iraqi exports through the Iraqi-Saudi Spurline, Nov 1986-Feb 1987
notes: Saudi Arabia, citing technical difficulties, restricted Iraqi exports through the 500,000 b/d Iraqi-Saudi Spurline to about 200,000 b/d -- slightly less than two-thirds of the 1986 average -- between November 1986 and February 1987.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.15

subcategory: Arab financial aid to Iraq since war start, cumulative total
notes: Since the start of the Iran-Iraq war, total Arab financial aid to Iraq has totaled about $33 billion.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.16

subcategory: Saudi Arabia + Kuwait share of cumulative Arab aid to Iraq
notes: About $27 billion of the ~$33 billion cumulative Arab aid to Iraq has been given by Saudi Arabia and Kuwait.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.16

notes: Financial aid to Baghdad probably totaled about $2.3 billion in 1986.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.16

subcategory: Saudi-Kuwaiti Neutral Zone oil sold on Iraq's behalf
notes: Under a 1983 agreement, Saudi Arabia and Kuwait sell about 240,000 b/d of oil from the Saudi-Kuwaiti Neutral Zone and give the proceeds to Iraq monthly (though 1986 first-half sales fell below the contracted amount).
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.16

subcategory: Value of Neutral Zone oil aid to Iraq, 1986
notes: Neutral Zone oil sales on Iraq's behalf totaled about $800 million last year (1986).
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.16

subcategory: Saudi Arabia's own-oil sales on Iraq's behalf, average volume
notes: Saudi Arabia sold an average of 60,000 b/d of its own oil for Iraq last year, totaling about $250 million -- almost all of which was provided to the Soviet Union to pay for Baghdad's military purchases.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.16

subcategory: Value of Saudi own-oil sales on Iraq's behalf, 1986
notes: Saudi Arabia's own-oil sales on Iraq's behalf totaled about $250 million in 1986.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.16

notes: Cash payments to Baghdad in 1986 were about $1 billion, almost entirely from Saudi Arabia.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.16

subcategory: Saudi/Kuwaiti-financed arms-purchase payments for Iraq via Egypt, 1986
notes: Saudi Arabia and Kuwait have also paid Egypt for some arms sales to Baghdad; this aid probably totaled at least $200 million in 1986.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.16

subcategory: Saudi/Kuwait financial aid to Iraq, 1987 estimate
notes: Financial aid from Saudi Arabia and Kuwait in 1987 is estimated to total $2-2.5 billion, depending largely on the level of fighting.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.16

subcategory: Oil sales/cash-equivalent aid on Iraq's behalf, present level
notes: Oil sales on Iraq's behalf, or their cash equivalent, probably will continue at their present level of about 300,000 b/d at least until the second Iraq-Turkey pipeline starts operation.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.16

notes: Iraq is estimated to boost its nonoil exports by one-third in 1987 to about $600 million, mostly from higher natural gas sales to Kuwait.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.16

notes: Iraq's 1986 nonoil exports -- predominantly dates, fruits and vegetables, natural gas, and industrial products -- totaled nearly $450 million, about 6 percent of total export revenues.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.16

subcategory: Iraq nonoil exports share of total export revenues, 1986
notes: Iraq's 1986 nonoil exports represented about 6 percent of total export revenues.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.16

subcategory: Iraq natural gas exports to Kuwait via new pipeline
notes: Baghdad is exporting 200 million cubic feet per day of natural gas to Kuwait through a new pipeline (financed by Kuwait) from the Ar Rumaylah oilfield to Kuwait's Shuaiba industrial area; Baghdad plans to double this and begin exporting about 40,000 b/d of petroleum products through a parallel line in 1987.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.16

subcategory: Iraq planned petroleum-product exports via new parallel pipeline to Kuwait
notes: Baghdad plans this year to begin exporting about 40,000 b/d of petroleum products through a parallel line to Kuwait, alongside doubling natural gas exports.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.16

subcategory: Iran foreign trade share -- Islamic countries, January-June 1986
notes: Figure 12 pie chart, Jan-Jun 1986 panel: Islamic countries' share of Iran's foreign trade rose to 16.0 percent (East Bloc / Non-Islamic LDCs / West segment values not legibly printed on the rendered scan and are left blank per the no-guessing rule).
citation: CIA NESA 87-10025, Figure 12 "Iran: Share of Foreign Trade, 1983 and January-June 1986" (percent, pie chart; source: Iranian Ministry of Commerce), May 1987, PDF p.22

subcategory: Iraq OPEC-assigned quota rejected by Baghdad, Dec 1986
notes: Baghdad refused in December 1986 to accept its OPEC-assigned quota of 1.46 million b/d, insisting instead on a quota equal to Iran's 2.25 million b/d.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.25

subcategory: Iraq's demanded OPEC quota (claimed parity with Iran)
notes: Baghdad's insistence on a quota equal to Iran's -- 2.25 million b/d -- has drawn increasing criticism from other OPEC members.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.25

subcategory: Decline in Japan/W.Germany/UK/Italy/France exports to Iran+Iraq, Q3 1986 vs Q3 1985
notes: Import cuts imposed by Iran and Iraq cut exports from Japan, West Germany, the UK, Italy, and France (together about half of all Iran+Iraq imports in 1985) by about 30 percent in Q3 1986 versus Q3 1985.
citation: CIA Directorate of Intelligence, NESA 87-10025, "Iran-Iraq: Gloomy Economic Prospects" (information as of 1 April 1987), May 1987, PDF p.26
